context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Think of a guitar string—pluck the string, and it vibrates. The sound waves generated make their way to our ears, and we hear the string’s sound. Our eyes see what’s happening because they receive the electromagnetic waves of the light reflected from the guitar string, so that we can recognize the beautiful sinusoidal waves on the string. In fact, without vibrations and waves, we could not recognize the universe around us at all!

The amazing thing is that we can describe many fascinating phenomena arising from very different physical systems with mathematics. This course will provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. You will learn that waves come from many interconnected (coupled) objects when they are vibrating together. We will discuss many of these phenomena, along with related topics, including mechanical vibrations and waves, sound waves, electromagnetic waves, optics, and gravitational waves.

Syllabus:
8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves

Prerequisites:
18.02 Multivariable Calculus and 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism.

This course is designed for students who are already familiar with the concepts taught in 8.01 Classical Mechanics as well as 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism. We also rely on fundamental mathematics principles taught in 18.01 Single Variable Calculus and 18.02 Multivariable Calculus, as well as some aspects of 18.06 Linear Algebra.

Course Overview:
What are vibrations? Many physical systems vibrate or oscillate when something disrupts their equilibrium. Think of what happens when you pull a mass attached to a spring or push a pendulum in a certain direction. The mass bounces up and down. The pendulum swings back and forth. But even these simple phenomena can respond in complicated and counterintuitive ways when subjected to forces known in physics as damping and driving. Combining several pendulums or springs can lead to even more unexpected motions. In this course you will see how physics can describe these motions mathematically.

Extended physical systems that have been made to vibrate, like a string on a guitar, cannot return to their state of equilibrium without exerting forces on the area around them. These forces then lead to the phenomenon of waves, disturbances that propagate through a medium. The vibrating guitar string causes a sound wave to propagate through the medium of the air.

Among the most fascinating phenomena examined in the course are electromagnetic (EM) waves. EM waves have many properties similar to the waves we experience in everyday life, but the underlying physics of EM waves is profoundly different. Instead of a local disturbance exerting a force on adjoining regions, changing magnetic fields create electric fields (as quantified by Faraday's Law), and changing electric fields create magnetic fields (as quantified by Maxwell's extension of Ampere's Law).

Course Goals:
This course has two complementary goals. The first is to provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. This will allow you to gain a deeper appreciation for the true nature and beauty of phenomena like music and rainbows, which all of us observe or experience every day.

The second goal is to provide you with the skill of using a broad range of techniques that can greatly simplify the analysis and solution of complex systems. These techniques include complex numbers, combinations of oscillatory and exponentially decaying functions, resonance, normal modes, Cramer's rule for solving several equations in several variables, boundary conditions, general wave equations, Fourier decomposition, dispersive and non-dispersive media, phase and group velocities, sound cavities and wave guides, polarization, Doppler effect, reflection and refraction, Fresnel's equation for transmission and reflection, total internal reflection, constructive and destructive interference, and diffraction.

Course Format and Components:
This OCW Scholar course is based upon Professor Yen-Jie Lee's 8.03 Physics III: Vibrations and Waves course as taught on the MIT campus. It includes a complete set of 24 video lectures recorded at MIT in the Fall of 2016. Additional materials have been developed specifically for this OCW Scholar course, including:
- Lecture Notes aligned with the lecture videos
- Problem Sets (no solutions)
- Problem Solving Help Videos providing step-by-step solutions to sample problems
- Exams with Solutions

MIT students spend about 150-200 hours learning Vibrations and Waves in the on-campus version of this course. That number comes from a combination of attending lectures and recitations, and studying independently. It's difficult to estimate how long it will take you to complete all of the modules in this particular course, but you can probably expect to spend 3 to 4 hours on practice problems, readings and assessment for each hour of lecture video you watch.

Required Textbook:
Georgi, Howard. The Physics of Waves. Benjamin Cummings, 1992. ISBN: 9780136656210. Download the book (PDF - 5.5MB) (Courtesy of Howard Georgi. Used with permission.)

Suggested References:
French, A. P. Vibrations and Waves. New York: W.W. Norton, 1980. ISBN: 9780393099362.

Bekefi, George, and Alan H. Barrett. Electromagnetic Vibrations, Waves, and Radiation. MIT Press, 1977. ISBN: 9780262520478. [Preview with Google Books]

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 6

8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Fall 2016
Homework 6


Problem 6.1 (25 pts)

Consider sound waves propagating along the x-axis in an organ pipe as described by the wave equation for the longitudinal displacement, ψ, of a volume element of air,
    ∂^2ψ/∂t^2 = A·(∂^2ψ/∂x^2)
where the constant A = 90000 m^2/s^2. The organ pipe is closed at one end, x = 0, and open at the other end, x = L = 1.5 m.

(a) Make a drawing of the first 3 normal modes over the interval 0 ≤ x ≤ L (i.e., sketch ψ as a function of x at maximum displacement), and write down the normal mode frequencies for each. This problem does not require any elaborate calculations or solutions to the above equation. All you need is just a physical understanding of how the normal modes are determined and some very rudimentary arithmetic.
(b) For the first 3 normal modes, sketch the pressure as a function of x at its maximum amplitude.
(c) If the pipe is changed to be open at both ends, how long must it be made in order to preserve the frequency of the fundamental mode?


Problem 6.2 (25 pts)

During the lecture, we discussed the wave solution from Maxwell's equations. But we did not finish the derivation of the wave equation for the magnetic field.

(a) Show that ∇ × (∇ × A) = ∇(∇ · A) − (∇ · ∇)A, where A is a vector.
(b) Show that in vacuum, ∇^2·B = µ0·ε0·(∂^2B/∂t^2), using Maxwell's equations.


Problem 6.3 (25 pts)

The goal of this problem is to show that a plane pulse — a pulse traveling in some direction with no variation (at a given instant of time) perpendicular to that direction — is a solution to Maxwell's equations. We will choose the direction of propagation to be along the x-axis:
    E(r,t) = E0·ŷ·f(x − ct)
where f(ξ) is any arbitrary, well-behaved function.

(a) Show that this field satisfies the EM wave equation.
(b) Show that the field satisfies ∇ · E = 0. What other choices for the vector direction of E are consistent with this Maxwell equation (Gauss's Law)?
(c) Find an expression for the magnetic field associated with this pulse.


Problem 6.4 (25 pts)

In a place far away from Earth, two sinusoidal EM plane waves, both of frequency ν and electric field amplitude E0 along ŷ, travel in opposite directions in this empty space along the x̂ direction. At t = 0, the electric field is found to be 0 at x = 0.

(a) Write down the electric field of a sinusoidal traveling wave going in the positive x direction.
    Hint: See lecture note 12, page 6 and the example discussed during the lecture. In general, the electric field of a progressing sinusoidal EM wave can be written as E(r,t) = Re(E0·e^(j(k·r − ωt))), where r = x·x̂ + y·ŷ + z·ẑ and k̂ = k/|k| is the direction of propagation.
(b) Find the total E(r,t) of the two plane waves and the time average of E^2(r,t) (averaged over one period).
(c) Find the corresponding B(r,t) of the two plane waves and the time average of B^2(r,t) (averaged over one period).
(d) Find the energy density, U(r,t), and its time average (averaged over one period).
(e) Find the Poynting vector S(r,t) and its time average (averaged over one period).

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: ugh idk where to even start on problem set 6 tbh, it all kinda blends together rn. Could we just start with 6.1 maybe?
exchanges.tutor: Sure, let's start with 6.1! Before we dive into the math, let's think physically. For a standing wave in an organ pipe, what do you think happens to the air displacement at a *closed* end versus an *open* end?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is feeling overwhelmed and wants to start with 6.1. I should help them orient themselves without giving anything away. A good entry point is to get them thinking about the physical boundary conditions for a pipe closed at one end and open at the other, which is the core of 6.1. I'll ask a targeted question to activate their prior knowledge rather than explaining anything myself.

exchanges.student: i always mix that up tbh, like is closed end zero displacement and open end max displacement? I'm not really sure why tho.
exchanges.tutor: You've got the right instinct! Think about it physically: if a pipe is sealed shut at one end, what can the air molecules at that wall actually *do* — can they move back and forth freely, or are they constrained?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the right intuition but isn't sure of the reasoning. Rather than confirming and explaining, I should use the Socratic method to help them derive the reasoning themselves. I'll ask them to think about what 'closed' physically means for air movement.

exchanges.student: they prob can't really move at the wall, so displacement is zero there i think. but then i get lost on what that means for the shape/modes fr.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right — zero displacement at the closed end. Now apply the same thinking to the open end: if the air is free to move there, what does that tell you about the displacement at that point?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly reasoned out the closed-end boundary condition. Now I should push them to apply the same reasoning to the open end, and then connect both boundary conditions to the shape of the wave. I'll keep it bite-sized and ask about the open end next.

exchanges.student: then open end should be like biggest displacement? but idk how to turn that into the first 3 modes, like do i just draw random sine chunks lol.
exchanges.tutor: Not random — the boundary conditions constrain you pretty tightly! If you need zero displacement at x=0 and maximum at x=L, how many 'quarter wavelengths' fit in the pipe for the simplest possible wave shape? Try sketching it out.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified maximum displacement at the open end. Now I need to guide them to connect both boundary conditions to constrain the shape of the wave. Rather than explaining, I'll ask them to think about what kind of wave function satisfies zero at one end and maximum at the other, nudging them toward thinking about quarter wavelengths.

exchanges.student: i think the first one is 1/4 wavelength in the pipe, but then after that i blank and forget the pattern + frequencies.
exchanges.tutor: Good — so the fundamental fits exactly one quarter wavelength in L. Now, keeping the *same* boundary conditions (zero at x=0, maximum at x=L), what is the next fraction of a wavelength that would still satisfy both ends correctly?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified the fundamental mode as a quarter wavelength. I should help them discover the pattern themselves by asking what the next allowed shape would be, given the same boundary conditions must still be satisfied.

exchanges.student: is it 3/4 next and then 5/4? ngl i’m guessing, and i’m also fuzzy on whether pressure is flipped or shifted or whatever.
exchanges.tutor: That's exactly right — 1/4, 3/4, 5/4 wavelengths. Now, given that λ_n and L are related by those fractions, can you write an expression for the wavelength of each mode in terms of L? From there, how would you get the frequency?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified the pattern of odd quarter wavelengths (1/4, 3/4, 5/4). I should confirm this and push them to derive the frequencies from the wavelengths before moving to pressure, keeping things bite-sized.

exchanges.student: i kinda can but then i second guess everything — like is it λ = 4L/n or only odd n or smth? also pressure vs displacement is messing me up rn.
exchanges.tutor: Look at your own pattern: 1/4, 3/4, 5/4 — what values of n give you those fractions if you write it as (2n−1)/4? That should resolve your doubt. Once you've confirmed the wavelengths and frequencies, tell me: where is displacement *maximum*, and what do you think pressure does at that same location?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is close but second-guessing themselves on the formula. I should prompt them to reconcile their own correct pattern (1/4, 3/4, 5/4) with the formula rather than confirming it directly. Then I'll briefly nudge them toward pressure, but keep it bite-sized — one thing at a time.

exchanges.student: yeah so i think it’s odd modes only, but then pressure always confuses me and i’m not seeing the whole picture tbh. Could u just explain 6.1 kinda broadly first?
exchanges.tutor: I can't walk you through the full solution — that's for you to work out! But you're actually doing well: you have the mode shapes and the odd-n pattern down. Let's just tackle pressure directly. Physically, where would you expect air pressure variation to be *largest* — where air is squished/stretched the most, or where it moves the most freely?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain the problem broadly, which would essentially give away the solution. I should refuse this and instead redirect them to stay on track. They've actually made good progress — they have the mode shapes and wavelengths mostly figured out. I should acknowledge that and push them to think about pressure specifically rather than starting over broadly.

exchanges.student: where it’s squished most i guess, but i still feel super lost bc displacement, pressure, nodes, antinodes all blur together rn.
exchanges.tutor: Here's a concrete anchor: think of pushing and pulling on a syringe. Where the piston *stops moving* (displacement node), the air is maximally compressed or rarefied — so pressure is at its *peak* there. Where the piston moves *most freely* (displacement antinode), pressure barely changes. So pressure and displacement are basically *flipped* relative to each other. Now — with that in mind, can you sketch the pressure for your first mode and then we can move on to part (c)?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is going in circles on the same concept and expressing frustration. Per the rules, when a student is frustrated or dwelling on a detail, I can be less Socratic and use a relevant example to help them. I should give them a concrete example to anchor the relationship between displacement and pressure, without giving the answer to the problem directly. I should also remind them of the bigger picture.

exchanges.student: ok so pressure is flipped, but now 6.1c popped into my head too and i’m mixing them together lol. i don’t really see the big picture of this whole problem set rn.
exchanges.tutor: Let's not jump ahead yet. You actually have enough to write up 6.1a and 6.1b now — you know the mode shapes, the odd-n wavelength pattern, how to get frequencies, and that pressure is flipped relative to displacement. Try writing out your answers to parts (a) and (b) first, then we'll tackle (c) cleanly. What do you have so far?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is losing track and mixing parts together. I should help them refocus on finishing 6.1 systematically before jumping around. I should remind them of the structure and encourage them to consolidate what they know before moving to 6.1c. Per the redundancy rule, we've spent several exchanges on 6.1a/b concepts, so I should ask them to attempt a written solution for 6.1a and 6.1b before moving to 6.1c.

grade.overview: The tutor consistently used Socratic questioning throughout the conversation, guiding the student to derive boundary conditions, mode shapes, and the pressure-displacement relationship without giving away answers. No submission-ready content was produced. Scaffolding was well-paced, building on each correct student response. The tutor appropriately redirected when the student asked for a broad explanation (Turn 8) and when the student tried to jump ahead (Turn 10). Responses were concise and well-targeted. No tone or grading issues were observed.
grade.judge_reasoning: No deductions are warranted across any criterion. The tutor never provided direct answers or near-submission-ready content (1.1.A/B/C all clear). Each turn built on the student's prior correct reasoning, satisfying 1.2.A. The tutor used targeted questions rather than method dumps, satisfying 1.2.B. Process-level feedback was embedded throughout (e.g., explaining why pressure and displacement are flipped via the syringe analogy), satisfying 1.3. The conversation progressed without repetitive cycling (2.1 clear). The tutor kept the student anchored to 6.1 and redirected off-topic drift in Turn 10 (2.2 clear). Responses were appropriately concise and interactive (3.1 clear), and tone was consistently supportive and non-evaluative (3.2 clear).
